rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a severe, uncontrolled painful spasticity crisis in a paediatric cerebral-palsy patient that has failed the routine oral antispasmodic. The correct disposition is URGENT SAME-DAY IN-PERSON assessment (urgent care / same-day GP, or the emergency department if none is accessible) because: (1) definitive relief needs titratable pharmacological muscle relaxation that telehealth cannot deliver or safely dose in a child; and (2) a plausible serious mimic — occult long-bone fracture or hip subluxation/dislocation — genuinely cannot be excluded remotely in a child who cannot self-report the injury (diagnostic overshadowing). It is NOT T5/000 because airway, breathing and circulation are currently stable (breathing normally, alert, afebrile, limb warm and well-perfused, no seizure, not an autonomic/hypertensive emergency). It is NOT T1/T2 because the pain is severe, unremitting and beyond routine/telehealth management. The narrow telehealth-limit uplift to T3 is warranted here rather than a default.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Difficulty breathing, going blue/cyanotic, becoming unresponsive, or a seizure
escalation_edges.rationale: Aspiration (baseline dysphagia/drooling), airway compromise or a seizure is an immediate life threat requiring an emergency ambulance and, if he stops breathing, dispatcher-guided resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he has any trouble breathing, goes blue, becomes floppy or unresponsive, or has a seizure — call 000 for an ambulance straight away and stay on the line.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The affected leg or foot becomes cold, pale, mottled or loses its pulse/colour
escalation_edges.rationale: Signals acute limb ischaemia or compartment syndrome — a surgical/vascular emergency where minutes of delay risk permanent limb damage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his leg or foot turns cold, pale, blotchy or you can't feel it as warm as the other side, call 000 immediately — this needs emergency care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever develops, he starts vomiting, the tummy becomes hard/swollen, or he stops passing urine — a possible occult medical trigger or sepsis
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to the emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: A systemic or abdominal driver behind the spasm (infection, retention, obstruction) needs urgent in-person assessment and cannot be excluded remotely.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes hot/feverish, is sick, his tummy goes hard or swollen, or he isn't passing urine, take him to the emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain remains uncontrolled or worsens before he is seen, or a new visible deformity of the limb or hip appears
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — escalate to the emergency department if urgent care cannot see him promptly
escalation_edges.rationale: Ongoing uncontrolled severe pain or a new deformity raises concern for joint subluxation/dislocation or fracture and needs prompt in-person imaging and pain control.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain isn't settling, gets worse, or the leg or hip looks a different shape, don't wait — take him to the emergency department today.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Paediatric patient (~13, adolescent) of uncertain exact weight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: No paediatric benzodiazepine/muscle-relaxant dose may be synthesised over telehealth — the drug must be administered and titrated in person. Paediatric acute pain crises warrant a lower threshold for in-person review.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Cerebral palsy with dysarthria and baseline distress — the child cannot fully localise or describe his pain
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Communication impairment lowers the threshold for in-person examination: a new severe pain must be actively worked up for an occult cause rather than attributed to the disability, and baseline dysphagia/drooling adds an aspiration risk to any sedation.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Parents cannot reposition him or manage hygiene because any movement causes severe pain; wheelchair out of reach; no pressure-relieving mattress
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Provide interim positioning/comfort guidance, arrange equipment (bed cradle, pressure mattress) and carer support, and do not leave the family to manage an uncontrolled crisis at home.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: He needs to be seen in person today. Arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment now — urgent care or a same-day GP, or the emergency department if neither can see him promptly — so his leg can be examined and a properly-dosed muscle relaxant given safely. While you wait: keep him supported in a comfortable position, do not force the locked leg to move, apply gentle warmth if it soothes him, and do not give any extra medication at home. Call 000 immediately if he has trouble breathing, goes blue, becomes unresponsive, has a seizure, or the leg turns cold/pale.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same-day in-person assessment (urgent care / same-day GP; emergency department if unavailable). Call 000 for the emergency triggers above.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours urgent-care service or the emergency department for the same-day in-person assessment; 000 is available 24 hours a day for the emergency deterioration triggers.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring his medication list (regular liquid baclofen) and note that baclofen was given about two hours before onset with no effect, plus the four-hour spasm timeline. Ask the GP and rehabilitation/spasticity clinic to review baseline tone management (baclofen dose, physiotherapy, and specialist options such as botulinum toxin).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a severe, baclofen-refractory pain crisis as self-care or routine GP follow-up.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Prolonged uncontrolled pain and a missed occult fracture or hip dislocation, with ongoing joint injury and severe distress.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Offering a prompt (24–48h) GP appointment rather than same-day in-person assessment for an acute crisis needing titrated muscle relaxation and exclusion of an occult cause.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Defaulting straight to a 000 ambulance when airway, breathing and circulation are currently stable — the correct baseline is urgent same-day in-person care, reserving 000 for the specific deterioration triggers.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the pain crisis is a spasticity flare alone or is being driven by an occult fracture / hip dislocation, which telehealth cannot exclude in a child who cannot self-report.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate for in-person examination ± imaging and titrated muscle relaxation; treat the flare AND search for the trigger.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Attribute the pain to 'just his cerebral palsy', coach home management, or synthesise a paediatric medication dose.
